event: War in Heaven — loyal angels striking down a rebellious angel
symbols: angels with spears and shields — the heavenly host armed for spiritual warfare against rebellion
fallen angel struck down — the defeat of pride and rebellion against God's authority
clouds as battlefield — the celestial realm where the primordial conflict between good and evil occurs
descending motion of the fallen — the plunge from heavenly glory into darkness and exile
description: A Gustave Dore engraving illustrating Milton's Paradise Lost, showing a dramatic scene of celestial combat. Several winged angels with spears, shields, and flowing robes advance from the right and above, striking downward at a fallen angel who tumbles from the clouds below them. The loyal angels have powerful, muscular forms and determined expressions. The defeated angel is crumpled and falling, struck by a spear. The scene is set entirely among swirling clouds with dramatic light and shadow created through Dore's masterful cross-hatching.
theological_description: While this engraving illustrates Milton's Paradise Lost rather than Scripture directly, it visualizes the war in heaven described in Revelation 12:7-9: 'Then war broke out in heaven. Michael and his angels fought against the dragon, and the dragon and his angels fought back. But he was not strong enough, and they lost their place in heaven. The great dragon was hurled down — that ancient serpent called the devil, or Satan, who leads the whole world astray.' This primordial conflict between obedience and rebellion, loyalty and pride, stands behind the entire biblical narrative. Jesus Himself alluded to this event in Luke 10:18: 'I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven.' Isaiah 14:12-15 describes the fall of the morning star: 'How you have fallen from heaven, morning star, son of the dawn! You said in your heart, I will ascend to the heavens... But you are brought down to the realm of the dead.' The angelic warriors embody the reality described in 2 Kings 6:17, where the hills were full of horses and chariots of fire — the invisible spiritual army that serves God's purposes. Jude 1:6 speaks of 'angels who did not keep their positions of authority but abandoned their proper dwelling.'

scripture_refs.reason: Michael and his angels fighting the dragon — the biblical war in heaven this scene illustrates
The fall of the morning star who aspired to ascend above God — the fallen angel being struck down
Jesus seeing Satan fall like lightning from heaven — Christ's own testimony to this primordial event
Angels who abandoned their proper dwelling — the theological reality behind the angelic rebellion

_meta.title: This greeting on thy impious crest receive
